author: @ianbottom7396
text: Robert why don’t you do an exercise bike converted to produce power? I think it will be really interesting to have an understanding of what levels of power output an average fitness human can maintain comfortably without dying and perhaps improving their fitness. I’ve mentioned using exercise machines to colleagues before and been laughed at but I still think it’s a valid idea to replace a device that consumes power to a device that generates it.

replies.author: @Google_Does_Evil_Now
replies.text:
Nature is way more efficient so we actually generate and use very few watts of power, compared to recent technology.

As technology advances then we'll see it get closer to nature, but right now tech is very far away from nature.

And as for AI stuff, this is also very recent. Looks to me like it mostly tries to summarise and combine information into human readable form but it seems unable to accuracy check what it writes. Which is very bad.

Why no accuracy checks? Lol

replies.author: @justinterested5819
replies.text:
A healthy, fit Human can generate around 100 Watts for an extended period of time.
Its much smarter to use that power to ride a bike to work instead to driving a car. A 10 km trip takes around 0.6l of Gasoline. 1l of Gasoline contains around 10 kWh, so it takes 6kWh to do that Trip by Car (or 30 Minutes by bike). So 30 Minutes of Biking can save as much energy as doing 60 hours on an exercise Bike (20 hours If you factor in the losses of an gasoline Generator).

replies.author: @justinterested5819
replies.text:
​@Google_Does_Evil_NowThats bs. Nature is far from being efficient.
One hectar of solar panels produces around 800 000kWh, while one hectar of energy plants only produce 100 000kWh of Plant matter at best. 
To get useable power from plant matter, you need to subtract around 2/3 of that, so only 30000 kWh remain, while the solar panels produces electrical energy, that can be used with 90% efficiency, so the Solar Farm produces 720 000kWh of useable power, while a hectar of Biomass plants only produce 30 000 kWh.
It gets even worse for biomass if you need a living being (like a human or a horse) to convert it to useable energy.

replies.author: @ianbottom7396
replies.text:
@justinterested5819 you assume a lot and I’m quite aware of how little energy a human can produce in electrical terms. 
For someone living without power a modified repurposed stationary bike is of practical use besides which it can produce a small amount of energy rather than consume it.

replies.author: @justinterested5819
replies.text:
​@ianbottom7396
Thats called doing maths. 
One 400 Watt Solar Panel can produce 400 kWh a year as far north as central Germany.
To produce that amount of power with an Bike, you would need to do around 4000h of nonstop cycling. I would say its better to do actual Work and buy some solar panels (or gasoline) instead of wasting your time on that bike. That can be exercise as well.

author: @sailorstu
text:
Good idea,
Just thinking if you weighted the flywheel a little and install a freehub you could get it up to speed and relax a little as it continues to spin and change the battery.

For those trying to picture this, think of a bicycle.
When you stop peddling you continue to coast for a while using the reserved energy.

author: @burnthetv007
text: ❤️Marvellous❤️ I would love to do a pushbike one…fixed or even ride n charge ! I am hopeless with electronics but love a cog or 2:) . Can you suggest THE best way. I can try research honed from there! Maybe a clay batt or two( for a mobile home) Love your methods of energy explorations!

replies.author: @sailorstu
replies.text:
They have actually had something called Dynohub's since at least the 1970's and there are modern versions of them on the market.
They work great but unless you are having a wheel built anyway it probably isn't worth the expense.

However there is also a much cheaper and easier to install version of a generator that locks onto a tire using friction to rotate.
This version is terribly inefficient and makes peddling much harder.
Not to mention wears out the tire prematurely.

If memory serves me correctly these systems all pump out 5 to 6 volts making them ideal for charging USB devices.

Personally I would be tempted to mount magnets as close to the center hub as possible and add some sort of windings.
If you have them close enough you will generate some electricity.
However since it's experimental I wouldn't know how much.

You of course know, this isn't Free Energy.
You will have to peddle harder when generating any additional energy.
However with all but the second system I doubt you will really notice the difference.

replies.author: @burnthetv007
replies.text: @sailorstu I remember those hubs that made the bulb glow when you peddled. I’ve seen a lot of different types including magnets, washing machine motors, car alternators n the like. All had their merits.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: personally i think the best thing is to give something a go - when you are working on a thing it is amazing what ideas come to you and how you tend to find a way - I am not kidding mate - the best thing is to stop looking for the best thing and try something you like the look of

replies.author: @burnthetv007
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thanks for reply! Yes I agree! Aesthetics is most important . A once famous designer once said that everything in his house , either is useful or just beautiful! Essentially I was asking about the most efficient way to utilise a bycycle wheel (28inches circumference !!! to utilise OR hub) My immediate thoughts are the huge 28 inch wheel turning a 1 inch cog etc. The rest is pure genius I have no idea of!
